And Qatar Airways has just stopped flying altogether until it's safe. These hubs are at a complete standstill. To really wrap your head around the scale of this, check this out. The big three airlines in the Gulf, Emirates, Etahad, and Qatar. Together, they handle about 90,000 transit passengers every single day. That's like a soldout football stadium full of people whose travel plans have just hit a concrete wall. You know, it's easy to get lost in all the logistics and the massive numbers, but behind every canceled flight, there's a person. And there is an immense human toll to this sudden paralysis. This number right here, over 20,000, that represents the passengers who are currently stranded in the United Arab Emirates alone. It's really just a fraction of the global total, but it gives you a real sense of just how many people are caught in the middle of this mess. So, what happens to all those people? Well, in a pretty unprecedented move, authorities in the UAE are actually stepping in. They're making hotels extend people's stays, and they're reportedly footing the bill for food and lodging. That is a massive logistical and financial challenge to say the least. But for the travelers on the ground, the reality is just complete uncertainty. This quote from Christy Elmer, an American traveler stuck in Dubai, really says it all. She said, "We've had flights booked every day for the week, and Sunday was canceled. Monday was canceled. Tuesday's already been cancelled. Just imagine that. That's the exhausting reality for thousands and thousands of people. And don't think for a second that this is just contained to the Middle East. It's not. This is sending shock waves through the entire global aviation network. Think about it. With Middle Eastern airspace shut, and remember, Russian airspace is already restricted, all of that global air traffic is getting squeezed into these dangerously narrow and congested flight paths. So that nice direct flight from Europe to Asia, not an option anymore. Planes are now being forced to take these really long detours either way up over Turkey or way down south over Egypt. And the consequences of that are huge. These reroutes aren't just a small inconvenience. We're talking about hours being added to flight times, tons of extra fuel being burned, and a much higher risk of misconnections for passengers flying all over the world. Okay, so what does all of this chaos actually mean for you? especially if you have travel plans in the works. Let's break down exactly what you need to know right now. Seriously, if you're flying anywhere near this region or maybe even just connecting through a different part of the world, you need to pay very close attention to this next part. First, and this is the most urgent thing, government warnings. The US State Department has issued depart now warnings for a whole list of places across the region. We're talking 14 countries. And it's not just the US. Governments in Europe and Asia are giving almost identical advice. These are not suggestions, okay? They are serious, serious warnings. Second thing, and I really cannot stress this enough, do not just go to the airport and hope for the best. Airlines and airport authorities are literally pleading with people, unless you have been directly contacted by your airline with a confirmed operating flight, please stay away. You'll just add to the chaos and you will almost certainly be turned away. So, what should you do if you're affected? It's a pretty clear three-step process. One, constantly check your flight status online. Two, contact your airline directly to talk about refunds or rebooking. And three, know your rights. Most major airlines are waving change fees and offering full refunds for flights canceled because of this. Now, with any crisis, unfortunately, the scammers come out of the woodwork. So, this is a really crucial warning for anyone trying to get information or a refund. Right now, consumer groups are already flagging a huge rise in scams. You know, you might post on social media that you're stranded and suddenly a fake airline profile replies offering to help. They're just trying to steal your personal information. So, the golden rule is right here. Only contact your airline through their official verified website or their official app. That's it. Period. So, as the skies stay empty, the immediate chaos is pretty clear. But the final question that's left hanging is so much bigger. For the last 20 years, the world built its entire travel network around this central hub. Now that it's basically a no-fly zone, the real question isn't just when the planes will come back.

- How Ukraine Is Having Its Way With Russian Air Defenses: Kind: captions Language: en Zelensky calls them “long-range sanctions.”&nbsp; And while that term is not exactly accurate,&nbsp;&nbsp; it does not change the fact that a&nbsp; lot of Russian assets are on fire. Nor does it change how Russia’s annual&nbsp; Victory Day Parade, scheduled for this week,&nbsp;&nbsp; is significantly smaller than in previous years. But for us today, we have a bunch&nbsp; of maps to go through. Their common&nbsp;&nbsp; bond? They help make sense of what&nbsp; is happening in the war right now,&nbsp;&nbsp; and why Ukraine has slowly moved itself&nbsp; into a much more favorable position. Yes, it seems that the proverbial dam&nbsp; has been broken. Ukraine appears to be&nbsp;&nbsp; striking every Russian facility&nbsp; that it can find. All it takes&nbsp;&nbsp; is a bunch of these drones and a&nbsp; small runway to launch them from. The damage on the surface is&nbsp; readily visible on satellite,&nbsp;&nbsp; thanks to NASA’s fire detecting technology. It&nbsp; is a far cry from the earlier days of the war,&nbsp;&nbsp; when Zelensky was stuck in a bunker and&nbsp; Russia controlled the Ukrainian skies. No need for fancy missiles launched from a&nbsp; silo. No need for missiles launched from a&nbsp;&nbsp; frigate either, which is a good thing given that&nbsp; Ukraine is still doing this without a crewed navy. And while the media’s focus has been on tabulating&nbsp; the damage to Russia, we ought to go beneath the&nbsp;&nbsp; surface and ask the deeper strategic question&nbsp; of how this is possible in the first place. No, we are not going to do something&nbsp; ridiculous and explain why Russia&nbsp;&nbsp; does not build its refineries in a&nbsp; bunker like humans can retreat to. Instead, the focus is on air defenses, and&nbsp; why Ukraine has been able to cut through them&nbsp;&nbsp; in a way that may eventually get many&nbsp; a statue built to remember Zelensky. Part of the answer is what Russia held in&nbsp; its weapons archives before the war started. Part of the answer is the&nbsp; inability to rapidly move&nbsp;&nbsp; Russian air defenses through&nbsp; the streets of the country. Part of it is Russia’s vast&nbsp; geography, with refineries,&nbsp;&nbsp; depots, and factories spread&nbsp; out over a massive landmass. Part of it is the inability for interceptors to&nbsp; train their sights on their targets fast enough. And I know that it already seems like a&nbsp; jungle out there with all of the moving&nbsp;&nbsp; parts. But there is still another key reason&nbsp; that Russia cannot control the situation. Ukraine is careful to place many of its production&nbsp; sites underground, in cavern-like facilities. Hence the last part: Russia cannot strangle&nbsp; Ukrainian drone production in the cradle. So, today, let’s examine each of these&nbsp; five problems, in this particular order,&nbsp;&nbsp; which have collectively put Ukraine&nbsp; in a much-improved position. I want to start with a couple of the faster points&nbsp;&nbsp; before transitioning to the deeper mapping of&nbsp; the impossible situation that Russia faces. First up is a problem that will&nbsp; sound familiar to the West,&nbsp;&nbsp; but perhaps taken to a greater extreme. Much of&nbsp; the materiel preparation for this war has been&nbsp;&nbsp; to simply look at what was still in the Soviet&nbsp; warehouses and adapt them for the current fight. That has worked well for Russia&nbsp; in terms of finding additional&nbsp;&nbsp; munitions to fashion into glide&nbsp; bombs to then drop on Ukraine. But if may briefly step in the world of&nbsp; gaming, I am reminded of the adage that&nbsp;&nbsp; “There are no wrong threats. Only wrong&nbsp; answers.” There a variety of ways that&nbsp;&nbsp; you can defeat an opponent. Many of them&nbsp; have proper defenses. But if you bring the&nbsp;&nbsp; wrong defensive strategy to the fight, then&nbsp; any of those offensive measures can win. To transition from gaming theory to war practice,&nbsp; for decades, the Soviet Union prepared for a&nbsp;&nbsp; confrontation with NATO. As a result, the Soviet&nbsp; Union built defenses for fancy missiles and&nbsp;&nbsp; fighter systems. Well, fancy from the perspective&nbsp; of someone in the 1970s and 1980s. Point is,&nbsp;&nbsp; from a material efficiency perspective, one way&nbsp; attack drones are impressive. On the traditional&nbsp;&nbsp; military dimension, they are fairly basic.&nbsp; So Ukraine started off at an advantage here,&nbsp;&nbsp; building an inventory of cheap bombs to&nbsp; overwhelm more precise countermeasures. Basically, if you have been following the&nbsp; complaints about U.S. losses from Iranian drones,&nbsp;&nbsp; it is the same story, except we are flip-flopping&nbsp; perspectives from the broader war coalitions. The second basic point is Russia’s inability to&nbsp;&nbsp; proactively destroy Ukraine’s&nbsp; drone production capacity. Indeed, the easier way to stop your&nbsp; opponent from overwhelming you with&nbsp;&nbsp; drones is to stop the drones from&nbsp; being manufactured in the first place. At the start of the invasion, it seemed like a&nbsp; safe bet that Russia would dominate the skies,&nbsp;&nbsp; and thus bomb any relevant military site with&nbsp; ease—again, basically a coalition flip-flop&nbsp;&nbsp; from the U.S. experience in Iran. Well, the&nbsp; jury may still be out on how much damage Iran&nbsp;&nbsp; has actually suffered, but the Islamic Republic is&nbsp; certainly in a worse position than Ukraine here. Indeed, it turned out that Western-donated&nbsp; anti-aircraft systems were enough to shut&nbsp;&nbsp; off that route for Russia about four years&nbsp; ago. At least this time equipment from a&nbsp;&nbsp; NATO-Russia conflict was accomplishing&nbsp; what it was supposed to accomplish. Now, Russia has switched to a more distant&nbsp; strategy, featuring ballistic missiles,&nbsp;&nbsp; those aforementioned glide bombs, and its&nbsp; own one-way attack drones. Part of why those&nbsp;&nbsp; still have not shut down Ukrainian production&nbsp; is because the blue and yellow have a very good&nbsp;&nbsp; defense network. We have explored how this works&nbsp; before, so I will not repeat those details today. Nonetheless, one thing I have not noted before&nbsp; is the geography of Ukraine. Broadly speaking,&nbsp;&nbsp; going from Russia to Ukraine is going from wider&nbsp; to narrower. That makes it easier for Ukraine&nbsp;&nbsp; to concentrate its defenses and stop Russian&nbsp; munitions from completely wrecking the country. If that geographic point&nbsp; was unclear, that is fine,&nbsp;&nbsp; because we need to discuss the problem&nbsp; in depth going the other way. Indeed,&nbsp;&nbsp; let’s now examine the trouble&nbsp; with Russia’s gigantic landmass. Imagine that you have a ton of valuable targets&nbsp; scattered all across your country. And, to be&nbsp;&nbsp; clear, if you are Russia, then you do not have to&nbsp; imagine this. It is your reality. Traditionally,&nbsp;&nbsp; this has been advantageous because it means&nbsp; that the opponent must penetrate deep into&nbsp;&nbsp; your borders. There is a reason, for example,&nbsp; that the Soviet Union built its nuclear weapons&nbsp;&nbsp; infrastructure in the middle of Central Asia.&nbsp; But much of those built-in protections have&nbsp;&nbsp; been lost with the proliferation of&nbsp; long-range one-way attack drones. Now, the level one solution would be to just&nbsp; put a bunch of air defenses around each of those&nbsp;&nbsp; particular sites. But quickly you realize&nbsp; that (1) this requires a ton of resources,&nbsp;&nbsp; (2) any night that Ukraine targets&nbsp; just one of those locations,&nbsp;&nbsp; the rest of the defenses were essentially&nbsp; irrelevant, and (3) there are a whole bunch&nbsp;&nbsp; of lower-priority targets that Ukraine may&nbsp; want to hit that are essentially defenseless. So the level two solution is to&nbsp; bring all of your air defenses&nbsp;&nbsp; closer to border. If you prevent any&nbsp; drones from breaching the local area,&nbsp;&nbsp; then you also prevent them from&nbsp; striking any of those valuable targets. It turns out, though, that Russia plays&nbsp; more of a level one game than a level&nbsp;&nbsp; two game. And as tempting as it is to&nbsp; declare that we are witnessing another&nbsp;&nbsp; silly mistake from Moscow and call&nbsp; it a day, the real reasons are far&nbsp;&nbsp; more interesting and tell us of a key&nbsp; structural advantage that Ukraine has. Let’s all stare at this map for a moment.&nbsp; Suppose that Russia just wants to defend&nbsp;&nbsp; its border. Then a knee-jerk reaction&nbsp; would be to draw a defensive line that&nbsp;&nbsp; looks like this. Except Russia took a bunch&nbsp; of territory since 2014, so actually you need&nbsp;&nbsp; this. But also Ukraine can fly drones over&nbsp; the water, so really, you need this. Oh,&nbsp;&nbsp; but if Ukraine wanted to, it could get frisky&nbsp; and fly through Belarus, so you really need this. But it somehow gets worse. HIMARS&nbsp; was Ukraine’s MVP during the first&nbsp;&nbsp; couple of years of the war. And while&nbsp; drones have since overshadowed it,&nbsp;&nbsp; the best shoot-and-scoot artillery&nbsp; piece is still delivering the damage. The most plentiful type of ammunition that&nbsp; Ukraine has for it is the GMLRS missile,&nbsp;&nbsp; range 70 kilometers. Anti-air systems&nbsp; are a priority target for GMLRS,&nbsp;&nbsp; because if you can disable those defenses,&nbsp; your cheaper drones have free rein. To draw another comparison to Iran, think&nbsp; of this as using Tomahawks to allow American&nbsp;&nbsp; bombers clear skies over Iran, which can&nbsp; then drop JDAMs on the rest of the targets. Now, Russia does place some of its&nbsp; anti-aircraft systems within GMLRS&nbsp;&nbsp; range. It is unavoidable when there&nbsp; are valuable targets within that band,&nbsp;&nbsp; including large cities like Kursk and oil&nbsp; pumping stations for its energy export industry. But those exceptions aside, the radius for&nbsp; the bulk of the defenses moves out to here.&nbsp;&nbsp; And we are starting to see the problem with&nbsp; Russia’s geography. As compared to Ukraine,&nbsp;&nbsp; the country radiates outward. The further that you&nbsp; want to install a line of anti-aircraft systems,&nbsp;&nbsp; the more that you need to cover it. You can&nbsp; see why this quickly becomes a financial&nbsp;&nbsp; liability—especially considering that when include&nbsp; the waterfront, you need this long of a line. Yet we can make it one step worse. Ukraine also&nbsp; has access to ATACMS, albeit in significantly&nbsp;&nbsp; lower quantities than GMLRS, with worse prospects&nbsp; of obtaining more due to the war in Iran, and with&nbsp;&nbsp; less documentation of Ukraine using HIMARS to&nbsp; strike anti-air systems at those distances.&nbsp;&nbsp; With those caveats noted, though, ATACMS&nbsp; have a range of 300 kilometers. Thus,&nbsp;&nbsp; the true safe line is all the way back here. Now, Russia could get away with that if each&nbsp; piece of its network could cover a great deal&nbsp;&nbsp; of that line. But spoilers: each one cannot.&nbsp; Ukraine’s drones fly low, intentionally to stay&nbsp;&nbsp; off of Russian radars. Combine that with drones&nbsp; generally having a small radar cross-section,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you are starting to look at needing radars&nbsp; every 30 kilometers or so, unreasonably assuming&nbsp;&nbsp; that you have favorable terrain throughout.&nbsp; This is because you need the radar to locate,&nbsp;&nbsp; identify, track, and pass along the&nbsp; targeting information to an interceptor,&nbsp;&nbsp; all before the drone goes out of range. This all&nbsp; likely must happen within five to ten minutes. That is how Russia ends up calculating that it&nbsp;&nbsp; needs to protect its best facilities&nbsp; on an individual basis rather than&nbsp;&nbsp; create a single comprehensive system&nbsp; to protect everything in the country. Of course, things get one step worse for&nbsp; Russia. Resigned to not defend the line&nbsp;&nbsp; properly, Russia still does not have enough&nbsp; systems to secure the sites themselves. And thus you get the familiar game that we have&nbsp; discussed in the past, where Russia has to move&nbsp;&nbsp; its air defenses from location to location so&nbsp; that Ukraine does not know exactly where they are. One solution is to not literally place&nbsp; all of the equipment directly in front of&nbsp;&nbsp; the targets but also on likely paths that&nbsp; Ukraine might take toward those targets. The main problem is that the United&nbsp; States, as far as we can tell,&nbsp;&nbsp; is still assisting Ukraine with intelligence&nbsp; sharing on the location of the anti-air systems. That allows Ukraine to either&nbsp; sidestep them and strike the&nbsp;&nbsp; open targets or hit them to&nbsp; make future strikes easier. Now, it would not be surprising to learn that the&nbsp;&nbsp; United States has dramatically scaled&nbsp; back its intelligence sharing efforts.&nbsp;&nbsp; Remember, part of the reason the United States&nbsp; kept with that despite shutting down direct&nbsp;&nbsp; military aid is that it helped give intelligence&nbsp; analysts some practice locating enemy assets. Well, the United States has been directing&nbsp; those efforts to Iran over the last couple&nbsp;&nbsp; of months. And while the parties are&nbsp; in a ceasefire as I write these words,&nbsp;&nbsp; no one should be surprised if hostilities resume.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is therefore imperative that the United&nbsp; States continues to track Iranian deployments. In turn, there may be a bandwidth&nbsp; problem in continuing to support&nbsp;&nbsp; intelligence sharing with Ukraine. Ironically, though, it would be good news&nbsp; for the West to learn that the United States&nbsp;&nbsp; has indeed paused that support. Ukraine has&nbsp; shown no shortage of ability to keep striking&nbsp;&nbsp; high-value Russian targets during this period.&nbsp; Therefore, it would tell us that Ukraine can&nbsp;&nbsp; get by with its own intelligence networks,&nbsp; without the help of U.S. analysts. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; I would be careful about drawing conclusions&nbsp; regarding a complete lack of U.S. support,&nbsp;&nbsp; as Kyiv may still be receiving raw&nbsp; intelligence from U.S. satellites. So, what are our takeaway lines for today?&nbsp; Ukraine has a golden eye for targeting valuable&nbsp;&nbsp; Russian assets. Geography and logistics give&nbsp; Russia no easy solutions to the problem. And,&nbsp;&nbsp; one way or another, Ukraine has sustained&nbsp; the campaign over the course of the Iran War. - The Epstein Files | Part 3 Flight Logs, Names, and Legal Meaning: Kind: captions Language: en Among all the documents connected to this case, none have been more widely shared or more widely misunderstood than flight logs. Lists of names, dates, destinations. For many viewers, these records felt definitive, concrete, inarguable. But in law, meaning does not come from appearance. It comes from limits. Flight logs have very clear limits. A flight log is a transportation record. It documents who boarded an aircraft, where it traveled, and when the flight occurred. That is all. It does not document conversations, does not document intent. It does not document behavior. A flight log cannot tell us why someone traveled, what occurred at the destination, or whether any wrongdoing took place. In court, these distinctions are not subtle. They are foundational. Private aircraft logs exist for practical reasons. Fuel tracking, crew records, maintenance schedules, regulatory compliance. They are not investigative tools by design. When used in court, flight logs serve one purpose only, to establish that a person was physically present on a specific aircraft at a specific time. They are supporting evidence, never stand alone proof. As flight logs entered public discussion, attention shifted rapidly away from victims and toward names, public figures, executives, politicians, academics. For many, the logic felt simple. If a name appears, there must be guilt. But the law does not work on implication. A name can appear in a record for many reasons. Professional travel, social invitations, fundraising events, or entirely unrelated circumstances without corroborating evidence. Presence is not participation. One of the most dangerous outcomes of misunderstood records is guilt by association. In legal systems, association alone is never sufficient. Courts require specific acts, specific intent, and corroboration. Without those elements, accusations collapse. Public discourse, however, often moves differently. Association feels intuitive. Nuance feels unsatisfying. But intuition is not justice. Context determines meaning. A single flight log entry without surrounding evidence is legally neutral. To establish wrongdoing, investigators must show what occurred before the flight, what occurred after, and how actions connect to a criminal act. In Epstein's case, that chain was never fully tested in court, which means flight logs were left to carry more weight than they should. In a trial, flight logs would have been questioned. Witnesses would have been called, timelines challenged, explanations tested. That process is where meaning emerges. Without it, documents remain incomplete. This absence is not proof of hidden truth. It is proof of an interrupted process. As flight logs spread, media narratives simplified them. Lists became headlines. Context became footnotes. This was not necessarily malicious, but it was consequential. Once a name becomes associated with suspicion, disassociation becomes nearly impossible even when no evidence exists. This is why courts move slowly. Speed is the enemy of accuracy. Flight logs can support investigations when paired with other evidence. They can corroborate timelines, can confirm presence when allegations already exist. They can strengthen a case built on testimony and material proof. But they cannot initiate guilt on their own. In Epstein related cases, they were often treated as conclusions rather than supplements. That misunderstanding changed public perception dramatically. Legal meaning exists to prevent harm. Not just harm to defendants, but harm to truth. When documents are misunderstood, victims are overshadowed by speculation. Injustice becomes performative rather than substantive. This case illustrates that danger clearly. With access to records comes responsibility, responsibility to ask, "What does this document actually prove? What does it not prove? And who benefits from misinterpretation?" Understanding legal meaning is not about defending individuals. is about defending process. Flight logs feel tangible. They feel objective. But emotional reactions do not change legal standards. If anything, they demonstrate why those standards exist. Because when emotion replaces evidence, truth becomes collateral damage. Accountability does not come from lists. It comes from trials, from testimony tested under oath, from verdicts delivered in court. That is why one case connected to Epstein stands apart. a case that did reach a verdict. In the next part of this series, we examine the legal proceedings that did not end in ambiguity. A trial, a jury, a verdict.

- How Do Pilots Pass The Time On Long Flights?: Kind: captions Language: en For most passengers, long-haul flights are&nbsp; a matter of endurance aided by a few drinks,&nbsp;&nbsp; inflight entertainment, and trying to get some&nbsp; sleep between meal services. It is an entirely&nbsp;&nbsp; different matter for the people flying the plane&nbsp; as there are still plenty of things to do even&nbsp;&nbsp; after takeoff. Let’s look at what’s happening&nbsp; on the flight deck during these longer flights… Even though flight paths are decided upon&nbsp; before departure, pilots have to monitor&nbsp;&nbsp; weather conditions and decide whether&nbsp; or not to alter them during the flight.&nbsp;&nbsp; On long-haul flights, weather conditions are&nbsp; more likely to change from initial forecasts,&nbsp;&nbsp; with aircraft often encountering multiple&nbsp; weather systems. These systems can vary in&nbsp;&nbsp; intensity from slight turbulence to dangerous&nbsp; thunderstorms. Traveling at high speeds,&nbsp;&nbsp; pilots must be prepared at all times&nbsp; to alter the flight path if necessary. If, for example, they see a&nbsp; thunderstorm on the radar,&nbsp;&nbsp; they must first get the OK from air traffic&nbsp; control (ATC) to change their route. Clear air turbulence is another worry, and&nbsp; because it cannot be seen on the radar,&nbsp;&nbsp; pilots rely upon the information provided by&nbsp; pilots flying the same route ahead of them. When&nbsp;&nbsp; flying across oceans such as the Atlantic, one&nbsp; pilot's job is to communicate with the ATC while&nbsp;&nbsp; the other monitors a common air-to-air frequency&nbsp; that pilots use to communicate with each other. The entire time a flight is&nbsp; in the air, the pilots monitor&nbsp;&nbsp; all kinds of gauges to ensure that the&nbsp; plane performs how it is supposed to. Making sure that the temperature of the fuel&nbsp; is not too cold is another thing pilots must&nbsp;&nbsp; monitor. If the fuel cools down too much,&nbsp; its flow can be restricted due to waxing.&nbsp;&nbsp; If this happens, pilots can rectify&nbsp; the situation by descending, or by&nbsp;&nbsp; increasing the aircraft's speed (and,&nbsp; thus, its skin temperature). However,&nbsp;&nbsp; this isn't a particularly frequent occurrence,&nbsp; as engine oil helps fuel to retain heat. While one of the pilots monitors all the&nbsp; gauges, the other will be busy taking care&nbsp;&nbsp; of the paperwork. Before the flight, the&nbsp; pilots are given a written flight plan.&nbsp;&nbsp; Anything that deviates from this&nbsp; must be written down as notes. Outside of these important activities,&nbsp; long-haul flying can be very boring for pilots.&nbsp;&nbsp; During cruise phases, pilots will often pass&nbsp; the time by making small talk with one another,&nbsp;&nbsp; or doing some reading. Smartphone games and&nbsp; good old fashioned crossword puzzles might be&nbsp;&nbsp; additional forms of entertainment&nbsp; found on the flight deck. Finally, just like passengers,&nbsp; enjoying inflight meals&nbsp;&nbsp; is another part of the long-haul pilot experience. The pilots typically wait until the passengers&nbsp; have been served and then eat separately.&nbsp;&nbsp; They do this so that there is&nbsp; always one person at the controls. For long flights over twelve hours, there&nbsp; are one or two relief pilots onboard&nbsp;&nbsp; so that the two pilots who performed the&nbsp; takeoff can take a break and get some rest. There are just two pilots on shorter&nbsp; long-haul flights of eight or nine&nbsp;&nbsp; hours. The only opportunity they get to&nbsp; leave their seats and stretch their legs&nbsp;&nbsp; is when they go to the bathroom. Did you know about these long-haul duties and&nbsp; activities? Let us know by leaving a comment.

- Fractional Jet Ownership 101: Buy Time, Not the Plane: Kind: captions Language: en Money can buy almost anything, but it can only buy time in one specific way, private aviation. The problem, buying a jet is a logistical nightmare. The solution for the smart money, fractional ownership. Private jets look like luxury, but fractional ownership is really a math problem. Instead of buying an entire aircraft, you buy a fraction, often 1/16 to 1/4. That share gives you a fixed number of flight hours. The operator handles everything else. Here's the math of the sky. Fly under 25 hours, charter. Fly over 400 hours, buy the whole plane. But in the Goldilocks zone, 50 to 400 hours, fractional is the mathematical winner. The cost structure has three parts. One, the share price, upfront capital for the asset, usually recoverable upon exit. Light jets start around $500,000. Large cabin can exceed $6,000,000. Two, monthly management, pilots, hangars, insurance, the subscription to readiness, $7,000 to $45,000 per month, depending on aircraft. Three, hourly rate, fuel and maintenance. You only pay when you fly, $3,500 to $9,000 per hour. It's not cheap. It's controlled. Here's where it gets interesting. The 2025 tax law permanently restored 100% bonus depreciation for qualified business aircraft. Deduct the entire share cost in year one. The tax benefits can fundamentally change the math. The risk, depreciation. Unlike art or real estate, a jet is a depreciating asset. Values can drop 15 to 20% from market peaks. Don't view this as an investment, view it as an expense for efficiency. You're trading capital for hours of your life back. A jet share is a six or seven-figure line item on your balance sheet. Don't let it disappear into the background. Track the residual value of your share alongside your stocks and real estate in Kubera. See the cost of your lifestyle. See the value of your time. Private aviation isn't about status. It's about time as your most valuable asset. Fly smarter. Track clearer. With Kubera.

  11. @StreamlineCarry: This guy is able to assess and make these deals ONLY because he is a knowledgeable A&P. This whole thing boils down to “I, the mechanic know what it will really cost to get something airworthy and you the owner do not. I therefore can hype up the cost of repairs to the owner and convince them to take my lowball offer.

There was also a lot of hand waving about borrowing money to fix an airplane. A hard money lender that is lending so you can fix an airplane is going to crush you on the terms because there is big risk that the proposed repairs will not actually get her airworthy.

All of that said, this strategy can work if you are a highly knowledgeable mechanic with the ability to accurately predict the cost to get something airworthy. This could be a good strategy for flight clubs that have members who hold A&P and/or inspector credentials.

- Why So Many Airlines are Going Bankrupt: Kind: captions Language: en This video was made possible by Shopify—the platform behind Wendover and hundreds of thousands of others’ online stores. The last five years have the most the consistently profitable and financially successful years ever for the world’s commercial airlines. Including the 2019 forecast, they’ve earned more than $160 billion in combined profit over this period. With a world economy more robust and connected than ever, the aviation industry is flying high… or at least most of it. In these same five years, the world has seen some extremely high-profile airline collapses and the frequency of these seems to only be picking up. The trend truly began in 2017 when Monarch, a well-respected British leisure airline with fifty years of history, went belly-up. The very same month, Air Berlin, a massive airline with over 8,000 employees and 100 planes, also ceased operations. 2018 then saw the end of Primera Air—an airline that generated buzz but seemingly flew to close to the sun in its final days with its low-cost transatlantic flights—and Cobalt Air—a small Cypriot carrier. 2019, though, was the year of slaughter. Wow Air, a huge player in the low-cost transatlantic market, stopped flying. Aigle Azur, France’s second largest airline, stopped flying. Xl Airways France, a long-existing transatlantic airline, stopped flying. Adria Airways, a mid-sized Slovenian airline, Then, after fifty years of history, Thomas Cook Airlines suddenly too stopped flying in what entered the record books as the largest UK airline collapse ever. Worth noting is that the aforementioned airlines are not cherry picked names. It’s certainly not the definitive list of airline collapses in the past three years, but they are most all the highest-profile ones, and with these, there are two interesting patterns. Firstly, every single one of these airlines is based in Europe. Secondly, and perhaps more strangely, every single one of these airlines, with the exception of Wow Air, stopped flying in either the month of September or October. Neither the pattern in location or times of these collapses is a coincidence. There are very good reasons behind these trends. For the timing, you see, in general airlines’ most profitable season by far is summer since that’s when there’s the most demand. There are certainly exceptions to this rule. For example, an airline flying between the Northern and Southern Hemisphere a substantial amount, like Qantas, typically sees more more consistent month-to-month profits as it benefits from the demand in both the northern and southern summer. The seasonality of demand also varies significantly by region. For example, here are a few years of overall month-to-month passenger figures in the US. You can see that there’s a clear peak each August and a clear valley each February, with smaller, secondary peaks each year for Thanksgiving and Christmas. If you take a look at Europe’s month-to-month figures, though, you can see how relatively stable the US’ traffic is. Relying much more on leisure travel, which is highly seasonal, European airlines have to cope with winter demand being almost half that of summer demand. Airlines in the US, though, carry more business travelers, in proportion, who tend to book more consistently throughout the year and therefore they don’t have as much excess capacity in the winter. There are even regions like Asia-Pacific that see nearly no seasonality in their demand, but the main thing to know is that Europe has highly seasonal traffic which means the timing of when its airlines make their money is highly seasonal. For example, take a look at Ryanair’s quarterly gross profit graph. In 2018, they made $901 million from January to March, $1.4 billion from April to June, then an enormous $2.1 billion from July to September. The fall was then definitively their weakest quarter with only $877 million made. Essentially, they made 3/4 of their profits in half the year. The problem airlines then have, though, is cashflow. For the busy summer season, airlines will get paid for tickets in the months leading up to those flights. Therefore, most of the money for that season will get to them between March and August. It’s therefore a season that can typically sustain even the financially weakest airlines. Come September, though, bookings start to dry up and therefore so does cashflow. With that, airlines will start to struggle to pay off their debts, let alone the costs for fuel and other operating expenses. Once an airline starts to fall behind on their debts, it tends to be a vicious cycle. Quickly, they might have planes repossessed, which means they have to cancel flights, which means they have to pay for hotels and penalties and for rebooking and pretty soon, the airline won’t have enough money to operate and will be declared bankrupt. In Europe, unlike the US, there is little opportunity for businesses to restructure once bankrupt so, for the most part, that means they’re closed down immediately and indefinitely. As the aviation industry is so volatile and capital intense, these bankruptcies can happen impressively fast. In Thomas Cook’s case, there had been hints of its financial difficulties for months, but at the end of August, it seemed the company would likely be saved by a buyout deal. It only truly became clear that the airline was headed towards its end the very week of its collapse but still, everything went on as normal from an operational standpoint. On September 22nd, at 9:40 pm, the airline tweeted in response to a customer, saying, “our flights and holiday operations are operating as normal.” That was true… at the time. All the flights were taking off, they were still selling tickets, everything was normal from the public perspective. Behind closed doors, though, things were not ok. Five and a half hours later, in the early hours of the morning of September 23rd, the company’s last tweet went out saying, “we are sorry to announce that Thomas Cook has ceased trading with immediate effect.” All around the world, at the very moment of their declaration of insolvency, flights were quite literally boarding, crews onboard, planes fueled, ready to go. With the company now gone, though, everything had to stop. Those crews were not longer employed, that fuel wouldn’t be paid for, the planes were no longer owned or leased by the airline, everything stopped. Of course, once an airline collapses and its flights are cancelled, there is no longer an airline to rebook passengers onto other flights. In most cases, passengers are just out of luck, but in the case of the UK, their Civil Aviation Authority essentially takes the place of any UK airline for the weeks following its collapse. The CAA will either rebook passengers onto another airline or just actually run their own flights. Of course, the CAA doesn’t have their own fleet of planes. Rather, they have a number of airlines around the world essentially on retainer for anytime they need to run repatriation flights. This includes plenty of charter operators like HiFly, Atlas Global, Wamos Air, and more, but perhaps the most unique aircraft used by the CAA was a Malaysian Airlines a380. The airline dedicates some of its a380’s to charter operations and so, the day before Thomas Cook’s collapse, when it became clear that the end was nigh, the a380 flew empty all the way from Kuala Lumpur to Manchester. It landed a couple minutes past midnight on September 23rd, just a few short hours before Thomas Cook was officially declared insolvent. As this and plenty of other aircraft pre-positioned even while Thomas Cook flew its last flights, the CAA was able to start flying its repatriation flights the same day and many passengers were only a few hours delayed, even after their airline went belly-up. The Malaysian a380 was assigned to fly flights from Palma de Mallorca, but part of the difficulty was that, from Palma, Thomas Cook had flown an enormous quantity of flights to an enormous number of destinations in the UK—London, Cardiff, Birmingham, East Midlands, Manchester, Newcastle, Glasgow, and Belfast. Of course, it’s much more complicated to find eight small planes than just one large plane, so that’s what they did—they found the the largest passenger plane in the world. Every flight the a380 flew ran just the two hours from Palma to Manchester and then from Manchester, buses ran to Glasgow, Newcastle, East Midlands, Birmingham, and London. Using this system, with just one aircraft, the CAA was able to run up to three flights a day from Palma to the UK with capacity for nearly 1,500 passengers—the equivalent to almost seven Thomas Cook a321’s. This whole process went on for weeks, making for a lucrative payday for the world’s charter companies and an expensive bill for the UK government, but it certainly is easier than having hundreds of thousands of passengers stranded worldwide. Having a record-breaking airline collapse and then another record-breaking airline collapse in the span of two years is certainly not normal and it’s certainly not a coincidence. Part of the problem is that the airline industry has been so robust in the past decade that it has allowed weaker airlines to survive. Out of the bankrupted airlines mentioned at the start—Monarch, Air Berlin, Primera Air, Cobalt Air, Wow Air, Aigle Azur, XL Airways France, Adria Airways, and Thomas Cook—five were focused on long-haul, low-cost flying. This business model is a rather new phenomenon made possible, at least on paper, by smaller, fuel efficient, long-haul aircraft and low fuel costs but, outside of Asia and Australia, no airline has really achieved sustained profitability with this model. Air Asia X, Scoot, and Jetstar are really the only airlines worldwide that have seen long-term financial success flying long-haul, low cost. After fuel prices plummeted in 2014, these airlines entered a period of rapid expansion as they were able to undercut the incumbents prices significantly, but then, in 2017, fuel prices starting ticking back up and these airlines had a tough choice—they could either raise prices and lose some of the market share they had built up or keep prices the same and grow further away from profitability. Many chose the ladder and, as fuel prices continued to rise, the companies began to fall. Many analysts have also suggested that another contributing factor to this spate of airline collapses is overcapacity. Somewhat as a result of how successful and profitable the industry has been over the past decade, airlines have been buying planes like crazy and investors have been funding airlines like crazy. For a while, the rule had been that adding more capacity means more profits, however, the industry has likely found the ceiling to that rule. This is especially true in Europe’s market where buying an additional plane that you might be able to fill in the summer means paying for it throughout the long winter too, when it might sit empty. What’s for sure, though, is that Thomas Cook will not be the last to fall. It is only getting tougher to survive as an airline, especially in Europe, and each year, without fail, another big name too will topple in September or October—the latest victim in the world’s annual airline culling season. One of the ways that Wendover Productions functions as a business, and stays out of bankruptcy, of course, is through merchandise sales. We sell a few types of t-shirts for both Wendover and Half as Interesting which is a nice, predictable income source that we control. Creating an online store at first seemed daunting, but then we came across Shopify. Shopify is a super simple yet advanced solution to creating and running your online store. They are designed to be a partner to independent businesses as features like a logo maker, plug and play store themes, marketing solutions, and much more make it easy to succeed. For your customers, it makes for a great buying experience—one that you’ve probably already used as they’re used by over 800,000 business worldwide, including mine, you know, in case you want to try out the buying experience for a Wendover t-shirt. By going to shopify.com/Wendover, you’ll be able to try Shopify for free for 14 days—no credit card required—and then after that, pricing is as low as $9 a month.

- How to Buy an Airplane and Write it Off on Your Taxes: Kind: captions Language: en Nobody likes taxes—they stink. But here, taxes are very important to help people&nbsp; discover their aviation lifestyle potential. You’d be amazed at what’s possible for helping you&nbsp;&nbsp; structure ownership in a tax-advantaged&nbsp; way for that airplane of your dreams. So why are we talking about taxes today? Well, this is the area where many clients who&nbsp; are interested in being involved with aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; discover things they had no idea were possible. Many clients come in planning to buy an&nbsp; airplane with the money they have on hand,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then they discover that there are&nbsp; ways to structure business or ownership&nbsp;&nbsp; of the aircraft that could generate direct&nbsp; offsets to their personal income for taxes. Literally, you can end up owning the plane and&nbsp;&nbsp; paying less in taxes than&nbsp; you would have otherwise. Now, there are many details that go into this,&nbsp;&nbsp; and of course, I’m not a lawyer,&nbsp; a CPA, or even a tax advisor. However, I am a businessman and have spent 20&nbsp; years helping people discover these things and&nbsp;&nbsp; working with our advisors, so I do know a lot. The&nbsp; big-picture concepts are the most important part. The first thing to understand&nbsp; is that, in the United States,&nbsp;&nbsp; our tax law is actually very well-structured&nbsp; to support a desire to enjoy private aircraft. This is due to the business tax laws that&nbsp; give us access to what’s called depreciation. There are multiple types of depreciation,&nbsp; but they all have the same idea:&nbsp;&nbsp; giving us a way as a business to&nbsp; invest in something and write off&nbsp;&nbsp; that investment either upfront&nbsp; or in a very accelerated way. There’s something called bonus depreciation—that’s&nbsp; the one that’s so exciting because,&nbsp;&nbsp; depending on which tax year we’re in,&nbsp; it could be anywhere from 40, 60, 80,&nbsp;&nbsp; even 100% in years past, where we can take&nbsp; the entire purchase deduction immediately. Now, we’re not talking about the&nbsp; operating costs in that moment. When we say bonus depreciation, that means&nbsp; if we buy a million-dollar asset that is&nbsp;&nbsp; 100% used for business in a particular tax year,&nbsp;&nbsp; it could range from a million dollars being offset&nbsp; of regular income to maybe 60% of that income. It depends on the set of facts we’re working with. Then there’s something called&nbsp; Section 179 depreciation and&nbsp;&nbsp; also general accelerated depreciation. It really doesn’t matter what any of these things&nbsp; are called. What’s most important is to understand&nbsp;&nbsp; that there are ways to structure the actual&nbsp; purchase price so that it becomes a tax lever. That’s the big-picture concept. How it gets applied to which type of&nbsp; aircraft and which type of ownership&nbsp;&nbsp; structure is the nuance of how to&nbsp; best structure it for each client. Many people considering aircraft think&nbsp; that they have to have a business in&nbsp;&nbsp; order to enjoy business deductibility for an&nbsp; aircraft. And while that’s technically true,&nbsp;&nbsp; what they’re not aware of is all the&nbsp; different ways that actually owning&nbsp;&nbsp; an aircraft and putting it in different&nbsp; structures creates an actual business. One of the things we did at LifeStyle Aviation&nbsp;&nbsp; was to create our own sharing&nbsp; programs, called DiamondShare. This has been a successful program&nbsp; for us for, oh gosh, 15 years. Owners own the aircraft, members fly the aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; and pay for general access. Their flight&nbsp; hours become business hours for the owner. It’s a fabulous structure for enjoying the&nbsp; aircraft, structuring the ownership cost,&nbsp;&nbsp; and allowing the owner to enjoy maximum&nbsp; deductibility with minimum usage of the aircraft. Another structure we use&nbsp; often is called a leaseback. When we say leaseback, we&nbsp; generally mean to a flight school. Looking at leasebacks is a great idea&nbsp; and can sometimes be the ideal situation,&nbsp;&nbsp; because technically, these structures can produce&nbsp; profits, making it a profitable enterprise. Now, candidly, we don’t promote&nbsp; this as “buy an airplane,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ll make money.” You might&nbsp; make money, but what’s great&nbsp;&nbsp; about it is setting up this potential for&nbsp; profitability within a business structure. This means you’re now going to enjoy&nbsp; all the tax-deductible potential of&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft ownership for business purposes. Those that fly the plane pay the school&nbsp;&nbsp; for access, and every one of&nbsp; those hours is business use. Every one of those business-use hours drives that&nbsp; deductibility for the owner of that aircraft. Another variation of a leaseback concept is where&nbsp; the aircraft is put in a Part 135 structure. Part 135 has unique rules. It’s the same idea, but the usage profile of the&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft is managed by an entity in the&nbsp; business of providing charter services,&nbsp;&nbsp; flying that aircraft around and providing&nbsp; those charter services to individuals. Then that income flow comes back to the&nbsp; owner—a great business structure that can&nbsp;&nbsp; provide possible profitability&nbsp; and definite deductibility. Another ownership structure many people&nbsp; think of is partnerships. It’s a valid&nbsp;&nbsp; structure, especially if you happen to have&nbsp; a brother-in-law, a family member, or someone&nbsp;&nbsp; you already know you want to split an aircraft&nbsp; with. This can be an ideal structure for you. There’s flexibility in how it can be split for tax&nbsp;&nbsp; purposes, and there are unique ways it&nbsp; can be created, structured, and managed. Partnerships are typically the&nbsp; hardest to create and get into,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the hardest to get out of. We help clients&nbsp; conceptualize partnerships and implement them. We also provide guidance on the considerations&nbsp;&nbsp; they’d want to think about to determine&nbsp; if it’s the right structure for them. If you’re considering an aircraft right now, now&nbsp; is the time for you to dig into this information&nbsp;&nbsp; and find out if it could apply to your situation.&nbsp; And if so, which tax year should it be applied to? Take, for example, if it’s November. There’s time for you to acquire an aircraft, get&nbsp; full deductibility before the end of the year,&nbsp;&nbsp; and take full advantage. It might&nbsp; be the ideal time to do it, or you&nbsp;&nbsp; can plan for another tax year—whichever&nbsp; is exactly right for your circumstances. Having helped hundreds of people discover&nbsp; an aviation lifestyle and achieve it,&nbsp;&nbsp; I can honestly say that it is&nbsp; one of the most life-changing&nbsp;&nbsp; things you can possibly do if you&nbsp; have the inclination to want it. Let us help you discover it, explore it,&nbsp; and find ways to justify and pay for it.&nbsp;&nbsp; It’s doable, and nothing can replace what&nbsp; an airplane offers in private aviation. So we hope you explore it, discover&nbsp; it, attain it, and enjoy it!

  1. @navajojohn9448 [17 likes]: One thing left out of most depreciation stories and it is a biggy. When you sell the item you have to recapture the depreciation.  Buy a plane for $1m  and do 100% depreciation then sell the plane for $800k  that $800k is now one big lump of income with a probably a tax bill.
  2. @tfargher1 [9 likes]: Only works when you have enough profits to effectively take the deduction.  You can take the loss against profits but the loss can only be taken against your basis in the company.  If you put nothing in you can't write anything off against it as you have no basis to take the loss.  Once basis is gone you can't take further loss deductions.  So make sure you have enough profits to take the deduction and or basis.

- What Is Codeshare Flight? | Advantages Of Code sharing flight #codeshare: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Hello, I am the CMD of the state. I welcome you all to this video series. Today, let's talk about code share flights. What are code share flights? Should I book one? What are its advantages? And most importantly, I will also share a live example with you. Let's see what code share flights are. Code share flights mean a mutual agreement between two airlines. Let's take an example, if a flight is flying from London to Mumbai and if one of their airlines has to go to Nagpur, that is, from London to Nagpur, so if it is a British Airways flight, then what will British Airways do? They will say that I don't go to Nagpur, but I go to Mumbai. So, the client will come to Preeti city first and then after Mumbai, the client could have taken another flight if he has to go to Nagpur, but how will this happen? British Airways will say that I will book a flight from Mumbai to Nagpur for you. And that flight will be of Indigo or another Indian airline or any local airline. This means that British Airways has made an agreement with another airline. A mutual agreement has been made, and that agreement is such that when my client lands in Mumbai, whatever his baggage is, it will be directly transferred to the next flight, and then he will fly from Mumbai to Nagpur on the next flight. Now, this means that British Airways itself will arrange for the next flight, for example, if they have an agreement with India, then British Airways will also start the ticket for the next flight, and that ticket will also be shown in the PNR on the British Airways website. Now, let's see what is the advantage of the code share. First of all, when he lands in Mumbai, the most important point for him is that his luggage is automatically transferred. I am giving this example because this issue arises many times. For example, if a British Airways flight is late in landing in Mumbai, then the next flight will fly, which is the flight to Nagpur. However, if the British Airways flight is late, then it is their responsibility because they have booked that code share flight. While booking, they told us that they had booked your flight directly from Nagpur to Nagpur, but there would be a Nikki in it, that the Mumbai to Nagpur flight is operated by India, so after landing in Mumbai, we came to know that you need to run around for your luggage, so there is no need for all this because your baggage is auto-transferred, so you can easily board the next flight. Next, your mail plane which you took on the British Airways flight, the same mail plane continues in the next flight as well, which means you will be provided mail in that too. Now why do airlines do this because airlines do not have their own customer corner. For example, if an airline says that I am planning to fly there, Mumbai to Nagpur, I do not have a flight, but my partner agreement with which I have done with another airline is flying, so they also issue that ticket and you can fly easily. If you do not have clearance yet, then a flight ticket is showing on your screen. Look carefully, this is a flight ticket to Georgia or Bombay. Look at this, this is a Qatar Airways flight from Georgia to Mumbai. This is a Qatar Airways flight. The flight ticket that you are seeing is of Qatar Airways. So, it is not clearly mentioned in it that it is flying from Georgia to Bombay, but it is flying to Doha. Now, when this flight is flying, its plus point is that Qatar Airways flies to Georgia to Doha, but the flight from Doha to Bombay is of Indigo Airlines. See, this is called a code sharing flight. It is written here that this ticket is of Qatar Airways. This means that the ticket has been issued by Qatar and not revised. They have also issued the Indigo ticket. This does not mean that there is no Qatar flight from Doha to Mumbai. No, they will also have a flight, but it will not be on time. That is why they have made this agreement. Now, look, here we are seeing that Georgia is flying to Doha first. After landing in Doha, their baggage will be auto transferred to Indigo and they will fly back to Bombay from Indigo. This is called a code sharing flight. How will you identify whether your flight is code sharing or not? One of the most important points of code sharing flights is that the code is given in digits on the flight numbers. Look, the flight number you are seeing in Indigo has a four digit flight number, which means it is a code sharing flight. Now you must have understood how code sharing flights work, so you should definitely book a code sharing flight because your baggage is automatically transferred to you. Even if there is a flight delay, there is no issue, it is the airline's responsibility. And most importantly, think about it, if we had booked two different airlines, for example, if we had booked a flight from Georgia to Doha Qatar and a separate Doha to Bombay Indigo flight, and if the flight leaving from Georgia gets delayed in Doha, then your next Indigo flight is not going to stop for you if you have booked different flights, but since you have code shared, it is also Indigo's responsibility to get you to Bombay. Therefore, always book a flight. If you need this video information, then it is important to watch this video. Like and subscribe to our channel and if you want to book your next flight with the best deal, do contact us tomorrow and we will give you the best flight deals. Till then, thank you all.

- LLCs and Aircraft - Part 1: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] hi welcome to aviation lawn minute i'm greg reichel and today i want to talk to you about using a limited liability company or llc to own an airplane i'm frequently asked that question because folks have heard that limited liability com companies can provide liability protection to the owners and members of an llc and my first question back is who's going to be using the airplane if the owner or member is going to be using the airplane then the llc isn't going to provide any liability protection because the owner and member will be the operator of the airplane and under the law the owner or member then would have a liability regardless of whether the airplane is owned by an llc if however a third party is going to be using the airplane then the llc can provide protection to the individual or owner member when that third party is operating the airplane if you have questions or would like to uh discuss whether using an llc makes sense for your ownership or operation of the airplane please feel free to get a hold of me thanks for watching you

- How to Become a Licensed Aircraft Mechanic - Salary | Training | Career: Kind: captions Language: en How to become a licensed aircraft mechanic. If you enjoy working with your hands, solving technical problems, and taking responsibility for safety, becoming a licensed aircraft mechanic could be one of the most rewarding careers in aviation. In this video, I will explain what aircraft mechanics actually do, how much they earn, the training and certification process, and what your career path can look like once you are licensed. Overview. An aircraft mechanic, also known as an aircraft maintenance technician or AMT, is responsible for inspecting, maintaining, repairing, and certifying aircraft as safe to fly. Every aircraft that takes off must be declared airworthy. That responsibility often rests with licensed mechanics. You are not simply fixing machines. You are protecting passengers, pilots, and crew. To work independently in the United States, you must hold an FAA airframe and power plant license, commonly called an A and P license. Daily responsibilities. So, what does the job actually involve? On a typical day, you might perform scheduled inspections, diagnose mechanical or electrical faults, replace worn components, repair structural damage, inspect engines and landing gear, complete legally required maintenance paperwork. You may work in a hanger, on the flight line, or inside a specialized maintenance facility. Some roles focus on routine line maintenance at airports. Others involve heavy maintenance inspections where aircraft are stripped down for detailed examination. Attention to detail is critical. Even a small overlooked issue can have serious consequences. Salary overview. Let's talk about earnings. Entrylevel licensed aircraft mechanics in the United States typically earn between $50,000 and $65,000 per year. With several years of experience, mid-career mechanics often earn between $70,000 and $90,000. Senior mechanics, inspectors, or those with type ratings on large commercial aircraft can earn well over $100,000 annually, especially with overtime. Pay varies depending on location, employer, and whether you work for a major airline, a repair station, cargo operator, or general aviation company. Overtime is common in this industry and can significantly increase annual annual income. Qualifications required to become licensed. You need to complete three key stages. First, you must gain the required experience. You know, most people attend an FAA approved aviation maintenance technician school. These programs usually take 18 to 24 months full-time. Alternatively, you can qualify through documented on the job experience, but this route often takes longer. Second, you must pass the FAA written knowledge exams. Third, you must pass the oral and practical tests with a designated mechanic examiner. These tests assess both your technical knowledge and your hands-on skills. Once you pass all three sections, you receive your airframe and power plant license. Career path. Once licensed, your career options are broad. You could work for commercial airlines, cargo operators, corporate aviation departments, maintenance, repair and overhaul facilities, general aviation workshops, aircraft manufacturers, government or military contractors. With experience, you can progress to lead mechanic, inspector, maintenance supervisor, quality control. Specialist roles in avionics, engines, or structures. Many mechanics later pursue type ratings on specific aircraft, which increases earning potential. Pros and cons. Let's be realistic. Pros: Strong job demand, good earning potential, clear progression path, hands-on technical work, direct impact on safety. Cons: Shift work including nights, physically demanding tasks, high responsibility, exposure to noise and weather on the flight line, continuous regulatory compliance. This career suits people who are disciplined, practical, and comfortable working with procedures and checklists. Becoming a licensed aircraft mechanic is not just about passing exams. It is about developing technical competence, discipline, and professional responsibility. If you would like a full step-by-step breakdown of the certification process, exam preparation, and job search strategy, you can find the complete guide at aviationc careerguides.com. If you found this video useful, subscribe for more aviation career breakdowns.

- The Economics of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en This video was made possible by Curiosity Stream. Watch over 2,400 documentaries for free for a month at CuriosityStream.com/Wendover. Private jets represent an inconceivable level of opulence. If the average American were to spend their entire year’s salary to charter a Gulfstream G550 from New York, they would just barely make it to Utah, and yet, there’s a class of people who use these planes to fly not just from New York to Utah, but rather routes like New York to Beijing. There’s a class of people who will spend tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars on just one single flight. Now, there are a few reasons this is strange beyond just the sheer price of things. Chartering that Gulfstream from London to Dubai, for example, you’d end up paying about $55,000 at rack rate. Meanwhile, if you were to fly Emirates First Class, which is just, if not more opulent, you could fly between the cities twenty times for the same price. What’s even stranger about this kind of expense is that businesses, which are intended to maximize profits for their shareholders, are able to justify this enormous expense as worthwhile. So, when is it that paying $8,000 or more an hour to fly makes money? There’s a pretty simple way of figuring this out. Out of the 8,760 hours in a year, the average CEO works 2,716 of them. For a CEO that’s paid $1 million a year, that makes an hour of their time worth $368. Among America’s largest companies, though, the average CEO earns $15.6 million. That makes their hour worth $5,750. For the most part, though, private jets fly about the same speed as commercial planes so when flying a route like London to Dubai, the time savings come at the airports on each end. It comes from being able to arrive, get on a plane, and fly—rather than having to navigate one’s way through a busy terminal for a fixed flight time. But still, flying private versus commercial from London to Dubai would save, at most, about three hours in airport time. With the cost of $55,000 for the flight, that would mean the CEO’s time would have to be worth $18,300 per hour. That wouldn’t be true until they earned $50 million a year—a salary earned only by the upper echelon of CEO’s. But the truth is that, for the most part, private jets do not make economic sense when flying routes with plenty of commercial service like London to Dubai. One of the larger corporate jet fleets out there belongs to Walmart. Now, this might come as a surprise considering that this is a company so focused on keeping costs low. You see, Walmart is headquartered in Bentonville, AR—a relatively small city of 50,000. Their airport does have a surprising amount of service for such a small city with flights all the way to Los Angeles and New York, largely propped up the company’s traffic, but for the higher ups, commercial doesn’t cut it. That’s why the company has a fleet of 20 corporate jets—the largest of any American company. These are most frequently flown by the company’s Regional Vice Presidents who are in charge of a specific area of the country and will have to make frequent store visits within this region. The company apparently has a goal that nobody spends a night away from Bentonville—they want as many trips as possible to be day trips. Now, let’s say that one of these executive vice presidents needs to take a trip to three stores—one in Rock Springs, Wyoming; one in Spokane, Washington; and the last in Great Falls, Montana. Getting to Rock Springs requires a seven hour itinerary through Denver that would get this executive in at 9:30 pm therefore already requiring an overnight stop. Then, the next day, they would do their store visit in the morning and, as there are only two flights a day from Rock Springs, they would have to wait until 4:50 pm to catch a flight back to Denver then another one to Spokane, getting in at 8:30 pm local time, therefore requiring another overnight stop. The next morning they would do their site visit, but once again, flight schedules dictate that the first itinerary to Great Falls would leave at 5:05 pm through Salt Lake City, getting in at 10:04 pm local time, thereby requiring another overnight stop. Following the next morning’s store visit, this executive would catch a noon flight to Denver and, after a three hour layover, another to finally get into Bentonville at 8pm. These three store visits would therefore take up four whole working days, but what if this executive flew private? Leaving at 9am, the first flight direct to Rock Springs would take an hour and 45 minutes getting in, with the hour’s time change, at 9:45 am local time. After a two hour store visit, the plane would take off again at 11:45 am, flying an hour and 15 minutes to Spokane, getting in at noon local time. After another two hour store visit, the plane would take off at 2 pm for a quick 45 minute flight to Great Falls, getting in at 3:45 pm local time. After a final two hours at this store, the plane would take off its final time at 5:45 pm bound for Bentonville. 2 hours and 15 minutes of flight time later, it would land at 9 pm local time, exactly 12 hours after leaving. What was a four day trip on commercial flights becomes a day trip on private, and that’s why Walmart decided private jets are worth it for them. It’s all about valuing the time of their employees and they’ve determined that, even for the relatively low level vice presidents, their time is valuable enough that it’s worth flying them private. For example, one of the aircraft Walmart owns and operates is the Learjet 45. It costs the company about $4 per mile to operate this aircraft including crew, fuel, insurance, maintenance, and all other variable costs. Therefore, the 2,900 miles flown on that day-trip to the north-east would cost them about $11,600. Saving three days, that places a value of $3,900 per day which means that, assuming the executive onboard works every single one of the 260 workdays per year, they would have to make almost exactly $1 million per year for this private jet ride to be worth it to Walmart—an amount within the realm of possibility for upper management at such a large company. Of course, that’s not factoring in the alternative option’s hotel, food, and airline transport costs which would likely sum in the thousands and it’s also assuming there’s just one passenger. If the plane were to be filled to its maximum capacity of nine, each passenger would only need to be paid $111,000 per year for the expense to be worth it to the company which is less than an average Walmart store manager makes. Now, there’s one other case where private jets can make economic sense over flying commercial. Let’s say Walmart was looking to expand into the Philippines. Flying business class, it would cost a minimum of $5,000 roundtrip per person, require three stops, and take over 26 hours to get from Bentonville to Manila. Flying private, though, a long range jet like the Bombardier Global 7500 could make it there non-stop, in just 15 hours, carrying 19 of the company’s top executives. Since the company does not own this type of jet, it would likely charter one at a cost of about $10,000 an hour, or $150,000 for the trip. While the cost of commercial airfare is less than this, assuming the CEO, who makes $24 million a year, is onboard, the value of the eleven hours of his time saved is worth $97,000—clearly tilting the math in favor of the private jet. The general phenomenon of globalization has been great for the private jet market as businesses need to travel to far off places like this. Especially as companies outsource manufacturing and other operations into developing countries, which don't necessary have much air service, many companies have determined that private jets are the best way to get where they need to go. But despite this, the private aviation industry was hit hard but the global financial crisis and still has not fully recovered. While part of it was genuine cost cutting, businesses also wanted to show that they were doubling down on luxuries by getting rid of their jets, even if they could make economic sense in some cases. It was all about optics and nowadays, these jets are coming with poorer and poorer optics, for good reason. Private jets are truly horrendous for the environment. If one were to fly that Bombardier Global 7500, the one that could make it from Bentonville to Manila, with just one passenger onboard, the jet would make it only to South Dakota before that passenger’s carbon footprint exceeded that of the average person in one year. Increasingly, these jets are even being used for purposes that cannot be justified economically. Since 2013, there has been about a 10% increase among Fortune 100 executives of using their company’s corporate jets for personal, leisure purposes. They apparently justify this by saying that, in case of a work emergency, they might need to get back to the office quickly and commercial air travel could hinder that. Firms that include this as a perk for their executives, according to one set of research, under-perform against the market average in terms of shareholder returns by about 4% each Of course, the real reason some companies might have private jets is not because it makes economic sense, because it quite often doesn’t. It’s because the people who decide whether the firm will use these are the very people that will use them. In many instances, the explanation is not economic, it’s societal. If that’s you or if you’d like that to be you, you should absolutely be subscribed to CuriosityStream. That’s because they have a library of over 2,400 documentaries and non-fiction titles that you can watch whenever, wherever. It’s available on the web, Apple TV, Roku, Android, iOS, and plenty of other platforms. One series they have that I would highly recommend is Innovation Nation with Mo Rocca who looks at new inventions in each episode and explains the stories behind them.   8. @theophrastus3.056: A good analysis for the most part. However the initial calculations (later corrected) are for a single CEO making solo trips. In reality, he’s usually accompanied by several other execs, also making top dollar.

  11. @jeremyleonbarlow: The economics are complex. I mean if you are living in Los Angeles and you have 6 or 7 buddies and you all want to fly to Vegas for the weekend, you can rent a light jet to fly you all collectively to Vegas and back for about the same cost as a commercial round trip ticket, give or take a couple of hundred dollars each per ticket. I mean the complexity of commercial ticket pricing gives a lot of wiggle room, but a realistic rental price for 8 friends flying LA to Vegas round trip on a light jet is between $500 and $600 each.

Renting a long range heavy jet or super mid-size private jet for a lengthy trip between cities for a business isn't just about the time savings for a single person in management typically. I mean a 15 or 20 seat private business jet probably has at a minimum ten people flying on it, so you need to factor in the price differential between X number of tickets and the costs of chartering or using a company owned jet, and what is more given fixed costs of an aircraft, even with high up front costs, owning a jet might be more economical still. This is because there are ways to improve the value of an owned aircraft which allows you to factor more than just the value of those folks' time savings at aurports. You must add to that calculus the fact that many businesses and individuals also subsidize company or personal travel on a private jet by triple net leasing their owned jet to a charter service with a deal that allows them 10 or more hours per month of usage at cost. This type of leasing arrangement is often through a service that allows them to use a network of jets in the charter service's fleet. This helps obscure travel by the company, protecting proprietary information in many instances better than traditional private ownership. I mean Taylor Swift and Elon Musk's tail numbers were being tracked as I recall and Sergei Brin and Larry Page decided chartering was more economical for this exact reason. Competitors knowing your next move by tracking where you flew is not a good thing, but owning and using a networked leaseback arrangement can be more economical in addition to obscuring your travel.

These leasing arrangements maximize cash flow on the jet as an asset to both the charter operator and the lessor by allowing the jets to keep moving and avoid a fixed base of operation. This maximizes charter revenue. This reduces the costs further for the owner of the jet by spreading fixed costs over more hours of usage and offloading those costs onto chartered guests. I mean with amortization and depreciation, a company which might have spent $7.5 million flying private over the course of five years, after the savings associated with the right charter leaseback arrangement, could have in fact spent just $360k for the same 600 hours of flight time. At what becomes $600 per hour for the flight time in such an arrangement, owning a private company jet makes a whole lot more sense.

Now it isn't always going to allow the company or individual to reduce costs that much, as some unexpected maintenance items might raise costs. I mean one cracked windshield could costs tens of thousands of dollars to replace, not to mention lost revenue from the loss of flight time while waiting for the repair, but it limits downside of ownership, and can still make flying private far more economical than traditional chartering. Of course chartering still might be cost effective for businesses or individuals who fly less frequently because of the value of time savings alone. This is why high usage owners can subsidize their travel costs.
