We want competition. That's Kindelberger, whose organization built the Saber pictures, the sky 10 miles high as man flies. The speed approaching that of sound. Today we're flying at very great speeds and at very high altitudes. As a matter of fact, up in the area at which a lot of the fighting is being done, around 50,000 feet, we have a different world. It's a thin, blue, dark blue air. The the sun doesn't shine so brilliantly because there's nothing to reflect it. There's no plan of reference for the pilots, such as hills or clouds or sky and such an atmosphere as this. Even a bomber is hard to see. And the trouble? That we are facing future is not the sound barrier. We know how to fly through that now. The thing that is bothering everybody is the thermodynamic barrier. The air flowing over an airplane at these very high speeds by friction will heat up the surface of the airplane. As a matter of fact, if we go Mach #2, which is twice the speed of sound at sea level, the surface of the airplane will get hot. Very rapidly and will stabilize at about 500 degrees, since ordinary aluminum alloy loses half its strength by 350. Degree. And that even titanium and steels begin to give trouble at 500 and 600 degrees, it's obvious that we are in a great deal of difficulty in the future. There also are many things like hydraulic fluid. We don't know how to make hydraulic fluid that won't boil away at this temperature. We don't know how to make packings it won't seize at this temperature, or bearings, or lubricants. In fact, if the bubble with whichever body is familiar gets soft, in which it loses its shape and disappear at 300 degrees. So we have ahead of us a great deal of research. And a long, long time of trouble before we're going to be going anything like the speeds at which our. Magazine supplement writers seem to think we are ready for tomorrow. And part of being a pilot is dealing with emergencies as we've talked about and shaking off whatever happened that goes bad uh with you, right? So tell us about the emergency you had in that Starduster and and kind of something you learned from that. >> The main emergency that happened in that um in that aircraft was one of my partners in the aircraft. He was flying it around and we had just installed a inverted oil system so that we could do arerobatics in that and um to test it he you know flipped it upside down and flew around for a minute and flipped it back right side up. And when he did that, he noticed that the the digital gauge that read our oil pressure dropped to zero. But the digital gauge in that airplane was squirly. It was weird. And it would drop to zero at random times. And so he didn't trust the gauge. Turns out that one of the failure modes of this inverted oil system can be that the little shuttlecock that that controls um the oil flow can get stuck in one position or another which and you can lose oil pressure if you flip inverted. It gets stuck in the inverted position. You flip back um right side up and it's the shuttlecock is still stuck there and it shuts off your oil. He flew around in that thing for 45 minutes with no oil pressure. >> Do you think he could have flown it upside down and it would have had oil pressure? >> That's exactly how he would have need to needed to fix it. It would had to fly upside down. it would have gotten oil pressure and then fly right side up again and maybe it would have that would have cleared the problem or he would have had to shut the engine off and turn it back on which also would have been a thing to do. Um in this case he didn't believe the indicator which you should never have an indicator that you can't believe in your aircraft. Never ever ever allow yourself to be in this position. >> Agreed. >> We didn't have that discipline back then. We do now. Um and so he um ended up his engine seized up partially over Lake Wolfford uh which is uh what's the identifier for that? 8 CL1 Charlie Lima 1. And um this airport is insane. It is very it's pretty short. It's very narrow. It sits at the top of a mountain. There are some updrafts that happen that that are right. It's kind of like landing in Catalina. >> Yeah, >> it's the same that same sort of thing. The engine is not nobody gets hurt. The the airplane is not damaged. And it it it was a it was a miracle that >> I'd fly with that guy any day. >> Absolutely. Absolutely. The words cut through the terminal like a sharp public accusation. Two little black twin girls froze, clutching their perfectly valid first class boarding passes, their eyes flicking up to their father as if asking whether they'd done something wrong. Behind them, the morning lights spilled across the windows, but the air felt colder than metal. People turned, some whispered, and the flight attendant. She never broke her stance. One finger pointed at the children as if they were trespassers in their own story. The father exhaled once, slow, steady. The kind of stillness a man learns only after years of swallowing moments just like this. Hello everyone. Before we begin today's story, I have a small favor to ask. These passes aren't real. You and your kids need to move. The pause before your kids carried the weight of every assumption she thought she could hide. One twin gripped her sister's hand tighter. The other tucked her ticket against her chest as if it were proof of their existence. He knelt beside them, speaking softly so only they could hear. You belong exactly where I put you. Don't forget that. But the attendant stepped closer, her voice sharp enough to draw attention from the next gate. I said, "Step aside. First class isn't open for." She didn't finish the sentence, but the meaning landed hard. A murmur rippled through the crowd. A mix of unease, irritation, and something uglier from a few faces too eager to agree with her. The father rose slowly. No sudden movements, no raised voice. Just presence, controlled, unwavering, the kind of authority that didn't need to announce itself. "Scan the tickets," he said. They're valid, she scoffed, tapping her badge like it granted her absolute power. I don't need to scan anything. I know fake when I see it. Behind him, a junior employee, barely out of training, shifted nervously. He had seen those tickets flash green earlier. He opened his mouth, then closed it again when the attendant shot him a warning look. The line behind them thickened. A few passengers crossed their arms as if the family was the inconvenience, not the discrimination playing out in front of them. One man in a suit muttered, "Always trying to get into places they don't pay for." Another woman clicked her tongue in annoyance. The twins heard every word. Their father heard every word. He had lived this soundtrack before. But today, in this terminal, in front of his daughters, something in him refused to fold the way the world expected. He pulled a small earpiece from his pocket, pressed it once, and said quietly, "Rachel, we may have a situation." The attendant laughed, assuming it was a bluff. The crowd assumed it was desperation. But the man himself, he knew exactly what he was setting in motion. And the airline, though none of them realized it yet, was about to learn who they had just humiliated at gate C14. The young trainee had seen the green confirmation flash on the screen earlier, but fear pinned him in place as the flight attendant stepped even closer to the family. "Sir," she said, loud enough for everyone within 20 ft to hear. "You need to stop pretending. First class is for verified passengers only. You cannot just walk your way into it." Her tone carried the same dismissive edge that had been thrown at countless travelers who did not look like they belonged. The father did not flinch. He had lived through versions of this moment so many times that the script was predictable. Suspicion, escalation, humiliation, and then silence. Silence was what they always counted on. One twin held her sister's hand tighter as passengers behind them shifted, annoyed by the delay, ignoring the growing cruelty unfolding right in front of them. The trainee swallowed hard, stepping forward an inch. Ma'am, his tickets scanned valid earlier, he said softly, almost apologetically. The attendant cut him off with a sharp slicing glare. Do not overstep. You are not clear to verify anything without my approval. The trainee froze again. The father looked at him, not with anger, but with recognition. He had once been that young man standing in a room where he knew something wrong was happening, but the power to speak carried consequences he could not afford. The father rose fully now, pulling his shoulders back. His voice remained controlled. Scan the tickets again. The attendant crossed her arms. I do not need to. I know what is real. Her words echoed the same arrogance he had heard at 25 when a bank manager insisted his down payment had to be fraudulent, even after he provided every document they asked for. It echoed the voice of the hotel clerk who once told him people like you do not stay in suites like that, forcing him to sit in the lobby while others breezed past. His daughters were now watching the same prejudice that shaped every hard-earned victory of his life. He would not let this become their memory, too. He let the weight of the moment rest on her shoulders, the same weight she had placed on two innocent children without hesitation. When he finally spoke, his voice was steady and measured. We will address that shortly. The supervisor, desperate now, stepped forward. Sir, I can assure you we will handle this internally. There is no need to escalate matters further. Jonathan looked at him with a level, unblinking gaze. You have already handled it internally. That is the problem. Silence and dismissal are the only outcomes that come from internal handling. Before the supervisor could reply, Rachel's voice filled Jonathan's ear again. Sir, the board has confirmed they are ready to proceed with formal action. They are requesting your directive. Jonathan nodded slightly. Standby. The gates public address system chimed unexpectedly, turning every head. A message appeared on the supervisor's tablet so abruptly he almost dropped it. Internal termination protocol pending. A second line followed, waiting for executive owner approval. The supervisor's face pald. Mr. Marshall, we can correct this. We can undergo additional training, follow report, issue apologies, whatever it takes. Jonathan's calm deepened. Justice does not come from scrambling to fix a moment after you realize who someone is. It comes from doing the right thing before you know anything about them. The attendant's voice broke entirely. Please, I did not mean to harm them. I just thought they did not belong. Jonathan raised a hand gently but firmly. You thought they did not belong because of how they looked. That is not a misunderstanding. That is not an accident. That is a choice. A chorus of agreement rose from the passengers. One man spoke loudly. If he had not been the owner, you would have dragged his kids to security. A woman added, "Some of us travel with our children. You treated those girls like they were criminals." The attendant covered her mouth, tears building, but Jonathan did not waver. His daughters watched him with steady faith, absorbing this moment that would shape how they understood dignity for years to come. He lowered himself again until he was eye level with them. "What happened today?" he said softly, is something you will see in the world, but you do not have to fear it because you will always know your worth, even when others refuse to see it. They nodded, small but resolute. When Jonathan rose, the officers waiting nearby straightened instinctively. One of them spoke. "Mr. Marshall, we want you to know we will fully cooperate with whatever actions you take. What happened here was unacceptable." Jonathan offered a slight nod. "Thank you." He tapped his earpiece. Rachel, proceed. Her reply was immediate. Confirming termination of staff involved. Corporate compliance is documenting all verbal exchanges, visual footage, and procedural violations. Standby for system completion. The supervisor sputtered. Termination. Sir, please. You threatened to have my family removed from an area they had every right to stand in. Jonathan said, "You implied they did not belong because of who they were, and you did it without hesitation. The gate display blinked again. Staff access revoked. Termination in effect. The attendant let out a small cry, covering her face as her badge flashed red. The supervisor stared at his tablet in disbelief as his credentials vanished before his eyes. A murmur swept through the passengers. Relief, outrage, vindication, all layered together. Then applause began. Soft at first, hesitant, then stronger. Gate C14 erupted. Not in celebration of punishment, but in recognition of justice delivered in real time. Jonathan remained composed, not triumphant. He looked at the former staff members with a quiet finality. You were given power. You chose to use it to diminish others. That is why this is the consequence. The attendant sobbed openly now. The supervisor looked hollow, defeated. The truth had arrived with the force of authority they never imagined would stand in their way, and there was no reversing it. Jonathan gently guided his daughters toward the boarding door. "Come," he said. "It is time to show you where you belong." And for the first time that day, the twins walked without fear. The applause at gate C14 softened into a reverent hush as Jonathan Marshall guided his daughters toward the jet bridge. Each step they took seemed to restore something that the morning had tried to break. Each breath steadier than the last, each gaze forward instead of down. The passengers who had witnessed everything cleared a respectful path. Their expressions a mixture of apology, admiration, and heavy reflection. Behind Jonathan, the two terminated employees stood in stunned silence. The attendants tears streaked down her face as she clutched her deactivated badge. The supervisor stared blankly at his tablet, the harsh red of the revoked access notification glowing like a verdict he could not escape. But Jonathan did not look back at them. not out of cruelty, but out of intention. This moment was no longer about their downfall. It was about his daughter's rising sense of belonging, dignity, and truth. As they moved forward, one of the airport officers approached carefully. "Mr. Marshall," he said quietly, "I want to personally apologize for everything that happened. What you experienced was unacceptable. We appreciate the grace you showed." Jonathan nodded, "Gracious, but unwavering. Thank you. What matters most now is that my daughters learned the right lesson from this. One twin looked up at him. Daddy, she whispered. Were they mean because of us? He knelt beside her, brushing a gentle hand across her cheek. "No," he said softly. "They were mean because of their own hearts, not because of who you are. You belong anywhere you walk with confidence and truth." His other daughter slipped her hand into his. So, we can still sit in first class. He smiled. You can sit in any class you choose for the rest of your lives. As they stepped onto the jet bridge, passengers behind them continued talking in hush tones. A mother holding a toddler whispered, "I hope my child grows up understanding what fairness really means." A businessman shook his head, "Unbelievable. They talk to him like he was nothing and he owns the whole operation." A college student muttered, "This is why people record everything. Some truths need witnesses." The trainee, the only staff member who had spoken up, approached Jonathan quietly before he boarded. "Sir," he said, his voice trembling with both nerves and sincerity. "I am sorry I did not speak louder. I should have done more." Jonathan looked at him with kindness. "You spoke when it mattered," he said. "Courage is not always loud. Sometimes it is the voice that rises when silence feels safer." The young man swallowed hard, a mix of relief and gratitude washing over him. Thank you, sir. I will remember that. Jonathan offered him a firm nod before guiding his daughters the rest of the way down the jet bridge. The warm cabin air met them as they stepped inside, and the lead flight attendant, one who had not been involved in the incident, greeted them with a respectful, almost reverent tone, "Mr. Marshall, we are honored to have you on board. Your seats are ready." For the first time that day, the twins smiled freely, their small hands brushing the wide leather seats that had once been questioned, doubted, and nearly taken from them. Jonathan lifted them gently into their places. "See," he said. "This is where you sit because you earned it and because you belong." The plane continued boarding, but at the gate behind him, the consequences Jonathan had set into motion were still unfolding. Corporate compliance officers had arrived to escort the former employees away from the terminal. Their careers with the airline were over. More importantly, a mandatory bias review was initiated for that entire airport branch along with a formal investigation into past passenger complaints. Change was coming, not whispered or delayed, but immediate and undeniable. As Jonathan fastened his seat belt, Rachel's voice returned in his earpiece, gentler this time. Sir, the board wanted me to relay their statement. They stand with you completely. They will initiate a companywide review of all customerf facing procedures. Today will not be forgotten. Jonathan closed his eyes for a moment, letting the significance of that settle. Not because he wanted recognition, but because he wanted a world where his daughters would never again have to prove they deserved basic respect. When he opened his eyes, both girls were looking at him with pure admiration. Daddy once said, "You did not even yell." "No," he replied, smiling softly. "Real power does not need to shout. It just needs to stand tall." The engines hummed as the aircraft prepared for departure. Outside the window, the sun broke through the clouds, casting a warm glow across the runway. Jonathan took his daughter's hands and gave them a gentle squeeze. "Remember this moment," he said. Not because of what happened to us, but because of how we rose above it. And as the plane lifted into the sky, gate C14 behind them transformed from a place of humiliation into a lesson, a moment when silence ended, dignity prevailed, and justice stood tall without ever needing to raise its voice.

- How Airbus Surpassed Boeing To Become The World’s Top Plane Maker: Kind: captions Language: en Airbus and Boeing have been competing head to head for decades as the world's largest commercial airplane makers. The European based Airbus entered the market 56 years after its rival, and today, 76% of the world's fleet is made up of Airbus and Boeing planes. But over the last few years, Airbus has edged out Boeing for market dominance. Airbus is clearly in the lead and they have a good lead. And if you just want to measure that, it's by backlog of the airplanes. Airbus has made a steady profit since the pandemic, while Boeing hasn't made a profit since 2018. Airbus total backlog, which are planes that have been ordered but not yet delivered, is 8726, compared to Boeing's 5643, which represents billions of dollars in revenue and is estimated to take almost 13 years to complete. We've gone from back in 1978 where you would hardly ever see an Airbus airplane in the US, to now having well over 50% of the market here in the US. Since 2019, Airbus has delivered more planes annually than Boeing, which has been dealing with crisis after crisis, including two deadly crashes of its best selling 737 Max eight, which grounded that aircraft for nearly two years. It's also been dealing with significant financial losses, manufacturing issues and production limitations. One thing I want to be clear about Airbus was already winning the race before the Max ran into trouble because the A321 in general was winning share. Narrowbodies make up the majority of the commercial aircraft flying today. These planes are very versatile. They can do multiple short trips a day or across oceans and continents. Boeing's 737 was the world's best selling narrowbody until 2019, when Airbus A320 surpassed it in total orders. And depending on how many planes are delivered in 2025, the A320 could take the reigns as the most produced jet family in history. If you look across the board at the backlogs and indeed at and orders. Everything is fine in every category except for the A321, which is absolutely crushing it. Cnbc explores how Airbus came to be the biggest commercial plane manufacturer and whether it can stay on top. The official 737 christening ceremony took place in the new final Assembly building. In the late 60s. Boeing was dominating the commercial aircraft market. It had just debuted the 737 and the world's first ever widebody jet. The massive 747 European aerospace companies took notice. The biggest misconception about Airbus is that it was some sort of instant creation, circa 1969. It really wasn't. It was a merger of a number of European national champion aerospace companies that had been building planes since World War II or World War One, and the only way they could do it was by pooling their resources. Thus, Airbus. As more people took to the skies, airlines looked to operate bigger aircraft. At the time, all widebody planes had 3 or 4 engines. For reference, a widebody typically has two aisles, flies longer distances, and can carry anywhere from 250 to 500 passengers, while a narrow body or single aisle has one single aisle through. The cabin flies shorter distances and can have 100 to 230 seats. Airbus shows up in the 1970s saying, hey, we have a widebody plane, but with just two engines, and airlines sat up and said, whoa, two engines and obviously two engines that can take you the same distance as three are better because your maintenance costs are a lot lower. In 1972, Airbus introduced the first twin engine widebody jetliner, the A300. Despite having only two engines, the A300 could only carry around 250 passengers versus the massive 747, which could be configured to fit over 500. Not a lot of people saw the need for an intermediate sized jet, so production rates were 1 or 2 per month for quite a few years. In 1978, the Deregulation Act was enacted in the US, creating more competition, new airlines and expansion of smaller ones. As airline needs shifted in the 80s, the demand for cost effective short haul aircraft grew. They began to see the need for a new generation single aisle basically centered around 150 seats. Airbus had not yet made a profit, and the company needed a plane in its fleet to compete with Boeing's popular narrowbody, the 737, which had already been around since 1967. Airbus launched the A320 single aisle program in 1984. The workhorse of the Airbus commercial jet family is the A320. The A320 was one of the few commercial aircraft designed to compete against Boeing's 737 powerhouse, and has succeeded where others have failed. Its fuselage is a little bit wider than the 737, and some airlines have taken advantage of that to put in slightly wider seats. The A320 debuted with a lot of new advanced technology. It was one of the first commercial jets that used a sidestick versus yoke controls. This is still a key difference between Airbus and Boeing planes today. It also introduced fly by wire controls, meaning when you pull back on the Sidestick, it sends electronic signals to a computer to move the aircraft versus a physical cable. One could argue from a flight controls point of view, the A320 is a generation ahead of the 737. When it first came into the market, it was controversial. Fly by wire when it first came out into the market was something that was really just used in military airplanes. The Jets debut, however, ended in a crash during the first demonstration flight at an airshow in 1988. When the A320 was first introduced, it suffered a series of accidents, including some fatal accidents that killed crew and passengers Seizures, and that really stalled the public acceptance of the plane. It took a while there, too, to get traction, but when it did, well, um, it succeeded far beyond expectations. A320 just came into the world 20 years later and has that advantage in terms of just fundamental design differences to the airframe. By the 1990s, the A320 success would start to put pressure on its US competitors. Airbus, which was backed by European governments, made its first operating profits. Once Airbus was more market proven as an aircraft builder and US airlines started to see the performance results of the Airbus aircraft and other airlines fleets, they became more open to buying Airbus planes, and it was really the A320 that helped Airbus crack the door open into the US market and many other parts of the world. Basically, Boeing through all of this had 50% of the market. So what you basically had was Douglas, Lockheed and Airbus duking it out for the other half. Lockheed exited sometime in the mid 80s and then everyone. New McDonnell Douglas matter of time. In 1997, McDonnell Douglas merged with Boeing, forming the duopoly we know today. If you think about the evolution of Airbus and Airbus really started in the narrowbody market with no market share, edged out Douglas, and then continued to get share. The Boeing 737 family and the Airbus A320 family are the company's best selling planes and have been a crucial segment of competition. The list price for one A321neo is around $129 million. Competition was head to head when it came to widebodies, too. In the 90s, more fuel efficient twin engine widebodies were introduced. Airbus debuted the A330 and A340, while Boeing introduced its 777, the A330. They kept refining it at Airbus, it did pretty well, and then Boeing attacked with the 787 Dreamliner, and despite all the flaws baked into that development program, it did better than the A330 in the long run. Then, from 2003 to 2005, Airbus took more orders and delivered more planes than Boeing. That 50 over 50 formula kept persisting, and what Boeing was doing was effectively winning on profitability rather than market share. They were willing to say, all right, we're going to be 50%, but our margins are going to be 9%. Yours are going to be four or less. It was a formula that worked for them until, of course, the problems with the max. Despite declining sales for four engine jets, Airbus spent billions creating the biggest double decker quad engine commercial aircraft in the world and possibly the most identifiable Airbus plane, the A380. It was in service from 2007 until it ended production in 2021. The A380 is an engineering marvel. Unfortunately, in a way, the A380 success was eclipsed by the success of Airbus's smaller aircraft, namely the A330 family and more recently, the A350, which are twin engine aircraft that can hold 250 to 400, 450 or so passengers and operate ultra long range routes. And you looked at Airbus and you thought, wow, you're going to be tied for number one. You might even be number one. The only thing you could do to screw that up is launch a superjumbo for a market that's going away super fast. And that's exactly what they did. It cost them a decade. Today, Boeing still dominates the widebody market with nearly 56% of the global fleet. These aircraft are used for long haul travel like international flights. Boeing currently has a backlog of over 1500 widebody planes. The company has had multiple manufacturing issues with its 787 Dreamliner and the 777X, which has yet to be certified, is years behind schedule. In 2012, Airbus announced it would begin manufacturing in mobile, Alabama. You go back to 1978, and Airbus had a small office here in the US with about a dozen people working there. Roll forward to 2025, and we have a very large manufacturing facility that manufactures airplanes in mobile, Alabama. We build helicopters in Mississippi, we build satellites in Florida, and actually our presence in over 40 locations across the Today, Airbus biggest customer is Delta Air Lines. It has slightly more Airbus planes in its fleet than Airbus has had a different manufacturing strategy than Boeing. Theirs has been more regional. So they have a final assembly plant, a foul, as they call them in Asia, one in North America, one in Europe, where Boeing's final assembly has been highly concentrated in in North America. China was almost exclusively Boeing customer for many, many years by having a final assembly line in China. I think Airbus would argue that that helped them get some share in China. Airbus has been manufacturing in the US for nearly ten years. It's about to open a third production line, two of which are for the A320 family and the other for its smaller 220 jets it acquired from Bombardier in 2020. In 2010, Airbus announced a new variation of its best selling narrowbody called the A320neo, short for New Engine option, which promised a 15% less fuel burn than the previous generation. In 2011, Airbus had a record number of orders for 1419 aircraft, more than double than the year before. That same year, American Airlines, which had been strictly a Boeing customer, put in an order for hundreds of these new planes. And in 2019, the A320 family surpassed the 737 in total orders. Originally, the business case was for around 600 airplanes and now we are up to 19,000. Boeing's response to the more fuel efficient A320 was the re-engined 737 Max that debuted in 2017, but over the next few years, the manufacturer faced a series of challenges. In 2018 and 2019, two 737 Max eight crashed and killed 346 people. It was later found a flight control system known as MCAS had malfunctioned on both flights. The planes were grounded for several months and production rates were reduced. Then the pandemic brought air travel to a halt, and the company had a record loss of $12 billion in 2020. Airbus saw losses of over 1 billion in both 2019 and 2020, but returned to profitability in 2021. But Airbus has consecutively had more annual orders and deliveries since 2019, making it the world's largest planemaker. Then in 2024, a door plug flew off of Boeing 737 Max nine plane, leading to more production delays of its best selling jet and an annual loss of $11.8 billion in 2024. Airbus has profited from the challenges that Boeing has faced. Some airlines decided to place orders with Airbus for aircraft rather than And if you look at Airbus's backlog today, it's dominated by A321. Airlines looking to cut down on fuel costs have been turning to these more efficient, longer range narrowbodies for longer flights like JFK to LA or the East Coast to Europe. Of our single aisle order book or backlog, as we call it, 72% of the Airbus A321. And that's opening up routes and markets that they couldn't fly directly before. The economics with single aisles were so much better than the economics with widebodies. And the Airbus A321neo wasn't perfect, but it was the biggest and best single aisle you could get, and leading Airbus to effectively a 60 or 65% market share from the standpoint of both deliveries and orders and backlog. It's become the hinge factor between the two. Boeing doesn't really have a comparable airplane. 737 Max nine isn't quite as big. The ten is big, but it doesn't quite have the range. So where Airbus has done well in the market is they have a product that Boeing really doesn't have. The 737 Max ten has a range of 3100 nautical miles and can seat a maximum of 230 people, versus the A321neo, which has a range of 4700 nautical miles and can seat 244 passengers. But the Max ten has yet to be certified. The bad news is, given these delays, these many year delays and given the challenges, and also, frankly, you've got this attack on the administrative state in Washington, which is also toxic for Boeing's standpoint. These are resources they need to certify planes. And are increasingly in short supply. So there's a lot about Doge and the attack on the FAA and other administrative agencies. That's really not all good from the standpoint of Boeing's certification. In 2024. Airbus delivered 766 planes compared to Boeing's 348. Most of the revenue from aircraft is received on delivery. Boeing 737 Max production has been capped at 38 planes a month since January 2024 by the Federal Aviation Administration. That rate was 52 planes a month prior to the pandemic, and two Max crashes so far in 2025. It surpassed monthly delivery goals compared to last year. Airbus has faced production challenges to it, like the rest of the industry, has been dealing with supply chain issues post-pandemic and an engine recall for many of its jets. There was the Pratt GTF engine two years ago with the difficulties they encountered with the metal powder issue that is leading to a big recall campaign, and that is limiting the number of engines going for new fit for for us. Also last year, with production difficulties faced by CFM and their supply chain. That has led to a difficult end of 2024 and also some shortages at the beginning of this year. Airbus deliveries have been lower compared to last year, but the company said it still expects to deliver 820 new planes by year end. The medium term, it's hard to see anything that stops Airbus from controlling that all important mid market segment. We're really talking about a battle for the 2030s here. The 2020s have been decided. The industry could face other challenges like tariffs and a possible recession in the coming months. For an industry that's thoroughly globalized this is absolutely toxic. There's a lot about these tariffs we just don't know right now. But it's remarkable the extent to which the jetliner industry became a model of free trade. And the US competed and absolutely did great with an enormous trade surplus, and that's now at risk. Now, as for the airplanes themselves, yeah, the majority of structures are indeed imported. Even though Airbus now has manufacturing in mobile, Alabama, it still imports parts like wings and fuselages. The US exports six times more than it imports in aerospace products and components. And so the concern about tariff regime is that that is then going to impede in that supply chain. And it's going to add to the costs of the airplane. More expensive airplanes could mean rising costs for the consumer. Both the Boeing 737 and the Airbus A320 and their variations are newer interpretations of jets designed decades ago. The industry has been waiting for new technologies, engines and designs to be introduced. The A320 has been a platform that's been around for many decades. The next generation airplane will be a completely new platform. It's going to be based on new propulsion techniques, a new wing design, new materials, and also a more digitally enabled airplane. We hope to see that flying in the second half of the next decade. It is so much more complex to design and build a new airliner today than it was decades ago. Airlines are struggling with what is the right design, what materials go into it? What engines do we use for it? What fuel powers it? In 2022, Boeing's CEO at the time, Dave Calhoun, announced that it would not introduce a new airplane into the next decade. So on that range and payload envelope, they've really got nothing that competes with the A321neo. They need a new They absolutely need a new So that of course, told people that they should go and order an A321neo, which they did. There were 1300 Hundred sales in the following 12 months. So Dave Calhoun, in a lot of ways, was the very best CEO that Airbus ever had. Airbus hasn't announced a new clean sheet plane either, but at a recent summit outlined what technologies could potentially power a new single aisle aircraft. And despite Boeing's struggles, experts say it's not inevitable that Airbus lead continues. Airbus is just positioned better because they just have more latitude to kind of wait and see because of their product lineup today. Right now, things look fantastic next five years at least. But eventually someone is going to disrupt them and it might just be a resurgent Boeing. And for the first time in way over a decade, they actually have a really good leader at the helm. Experts also say China's Comac could enter the market. If Comac is able to make inroads in the West. Especially, the Chinese government decides to subsidize the sale of those airplanes to get some sales into Western airlines. That could introduce one of the most serious threats to both Airbus and Boeing that we have seen in a long time. Brazil based Embraer could also be a strong competitor to enter the narrowbody space. The company, which makes smaller regional and business jets, has been talked about as a company that could disrupt the duopoly. I'd put my money on Embraer. First, Embraer is a very, very capable company. Good engineers. If Boeing doesn't do a competitive response, then again, the 321 neo becomes the hinge of fate. Obviously, in the long run, a lot can go wrong with that. It's your classic innovator's dilemma. Why would you disrupt yourself when you're the overwhelmingly dominant player on the market? So will something change? It must. Entropy, right? But until then, Airbus gets two thirds of the market, which is not at all bad.

really good thing in my partnership has been shared goals it's been great that both of us in the partnership have similar objectives in aviation neither of us are on a path to the airlines or fly such locally neither of us has a reason to push for an autopilot a wasp gps or any other very expensive upgrades now i'm not saying that won't ever happen but right now our objectives are aligned well finally the thing that's helped in our partnership has been excitement around the same aircraft in every partnership there are compromises that must occur it's my opinion that the biggest compromise over the other now you might say if i owned the airplane myself all that responsibility would be mine which is probably true however as a sole owner you could make decisions without having to consult with your partner and you can eliminate some of the excess accounting work that is needed in the this has not been a major issue in our partnership but there have been plenty of times when one partner - 100% Bonus Depreciation: A Game Changer for Aircraft Buyers: Kind: captions Language: en Hi there, I'm JP Dice with Skybound Partners. Big news in the aviation world recently. 100% bonus depreciation is back. With me is KJ McCarter. He's with Aviation Tax Consultants. What does all of this mean for someone making the big investment to buy an airplane? It's a it's a huge deal for aircraft owners and purchasers to have 100% bonus depreciation back because it allows uh a much more substantial tax savings in year one for a taxpayer acquiring an aircraft. So, I know we can dive into the details uh here in a couple minutes, but it's a it's a huge win for the industry for sure. Okay. You know, I it's my understanding and the reason I I'm talking to you, you're the tax expert, but this is not for everybody buying an aircraft. You just can't automatically write this airplane off. It has to have some there's some rules related to business use. Correct. Correct. Correct. We have to be using the aircraft for legitimate, well doumented business use in order to be able to depreciate the aircraft to begin with. So this only applies to those taxpayers that are purchasing an aircraft for business use. Aircraft that are used purely for personal use, this 100% bonus depreciation uh uh law passing won't have any implication for them. Okay. So give me some examples. Uh and just thinking here somebody that hey I'm buying an airplane because I want to go on weekend trips to the beach. That's not it. But let's say you are a company and you have uh offices throughout the southeast or even beyond that and you need to get your employees from point A to point B to visit those offices or you need to to visit those those facilities. This is where this comes into play or maybe even visiting potential clients or customers. You hit the nail on the head. All all the above. uh companies that are acquiring an aircraft to fly around to their offices throughout the southeast or throughout the United States. Uh companies that are going to uh fly around to visit with clients or prospects or go to job sites or go give quotes. uh whatever that non-local travel need is. As long as it's a legitimate business need that the company has for the aircraft, that's who can benefit from this 100% bonus depreciation because they'll have the ability to write off their use of the aircraft for their portion of use that is uh for business. Uh give me a simple example. We'll we'll talk about a jet aircraft that say costs $5 million and and a company was going to buy this or an individual that has a company is buying this airplane. How does this work as far as what happens in the tax process? Yep. So, let's say it's a company that has a $10 million taxable bottom line before the plane. So, they were going to profit $10 million before they went out and acquired an aircraft. Let's say they go out and this summer they're on the hunt for a $5 million aircraft and they buy it in the fall. If they use the plane for 100% business use in 2025, for example, then we can depreciate the full $5 million purchase price of the aircraft in 2025 against that $10 million of taxable income. So taxable income goes from 10 million to 5 million after we depreciate the plane. and our tax savings. It depends on the the tax rate that's being applied to that taxable income, but let's just say it's as a napkin math rule of thumb, let's say it's 40%. We just save 40% of $5 million. So, $2 million bucks. Okay? So, a significant savings uh for a company that is going to be using an aircraft for for business uh because of this change in the law. Now, let's talk about something else that sometimes confuses people. Recapture. How does that work? Because on the back end, there's some recapture here. That is that is the caveat for that hypothetical. We save 2 million in 2025, but let's say we go and sell that plane in 2029. Let's say we sell it for 4 million in 2029. When we sell it for 4 million in 2029, our basis in the plane is zero. We fully depreciated the plane. So, our gain on the plane is 4 million. So, we have $4 million of ordinary income in the year that we sell the plane and we pay tax on that 4 million. Okay. So, uh is is there an advantage to at that point go out and we're we're buying a new aircraft? What what what kind of the guidance there that you suggest? Great question. And that is the normal strategy amongst our client base. We don't have like kind exchange for aircraft right now, but having a 100% bonus depreciation back kind of allows us to do a a similar get to a similar result really if we sell the plane the current plane in the same year that we acquire the next plane. And if those two transactions hit the same tax return in the same tax year, they can offset each other. So let's continue on with the same example. We just sold a plane for 4 million in 2029. If we go out and buy another aircraft for 5 million in 2029, right now we're slated to have 100% bonus depreciation in 2029 as well. So, we take 100% bonus on that $5 million plane. We wipe out the $4 million of gain from the sale of the previous aircraft and we have an additional 1 million uh of depreciation to offset other taxable income in that year. So, that is that is the common strategy. Buy and sell in the same year to allow the two to kind of offset each other. Okay. makes complete sense. Uh, one other question. If you're a student pilot, pilot who doesn't fly at controlled fields, or simply just rusting on the comms, talking to air traffic control can be intimidating, and the fear of messing up, being corrected, or not understanding what ATC just said can take the fun out of flying. But despite your fears, realize that ATC is there to help keep everyone safe and can be just as stressed as you are. Bottom line, the FAA requires certain communications to happen on the radio, and that puts pressures on the controllers. So, they have to put pressure on the pilots to make sure that everyone is in compliance. So, let's break communications down into things that are a little more simple, easier to manage. In radio communications, there's three parts. The request, instructions, and confirmation. So, you make a request to air traffic control. They give you instructions, and you repeat the instructions to confirm that you heard them correctly. Then, unless you've been asked to report back or you have a new request, you only receive and confirm updated instructions from air traffic control. That's it. That's literally all radio communication is. But because what you say and how you say it matters, we're going to dig in a little deeper by walking you guys through a flight. Now, before we end this one, I wanted to give you guys a few tips. First, don't feel stupid. Everyone makes mistakes. I still mess up on my call. I messed up on a bunch today. Just roll with it and shake it off if it happens. Avoid using two and four next to important numbers in your transmission because they sound like numbers, too. Use a single transmission on initial contact or when verifying new instructions. If you miss something, don't press the button again, then just add to it. If ATC asks you to verify, just say the whole thing over again. Speak at your own pace. Just because they talk fast doesn't mean that you need to. And if they talk so fast that you can't understand them, you can always ask them to say again or speak slower. Ask for help if you need it. Like if you can't find the traffic you're following, ask air traffic control to call your base. Don't be afraid to use this one, but if you're uncomfortable with their instructions or it would be unsafe for you to follow them, say unable. According to internal data from US Immigration and Custom Enforcement and the Transportation Security Administration, over 800 people were arrested between January 2025 and February 2026. Now, this has been going on a while. Hasn't been going on since this January. It's been going on since last January. For all those people who think that ICE has recently gotten in airports, they've already been in airports and they've always been in airports enforcing immigration law. And this information is based on tips from the TSA. Let that sink in. This wasn't random enforcement. This was datadriven interception. And even more surprising, TSA shared data on over 31,000 travelers with ICE. Ladies and gentlemen, that's not a handful of cases. That's a pipeline. Most people think TSA's secure flight program is about one thing, and that's stopping terrorism. It was created in 2007 to identify threats on government watch list. But now it's being used to flag individuals with final deportation orders, people with routine immigration violations. And here's how it works. TSA receives your flight info. The data is checked against government databases. If there's a match, ICE is notified before you even board the airplane. And when you land, they're already there. This isn't random. This is systematic. ICE receives passenger manifest multiple times a week. When someone flagged is flying, TSA shares the flight details. ICE tracks the arrival and agents intercept at the airport. No warning, no border crossing, just a domestic flight turned into an enforcement action. This is where it gets uncomfortable. These aren't just statistics. These are real people. A couple living in the US for over 20 years, detained in front of their kids on a domestic trip. A mother arrested at San Francisco International Airport the day before expanded ICE airport operation. A college student detained flying home for Thanksgiving. No international travel, no border stop, just airports becoming checkpoints. And this is where the debate explodes. Over 40 members of Congress have pushed back, saying this creates confusion and fear, a breakdown of trust in airport security. And critics argue that tools built for aviation safety are now being used for immigration enforcement. And that shift changes everything. The Department of Homeland Security says these individuals already had final orders of removal. From their perspective, this isn't new authority. It's just better coordination. But here's the real question. When you book a flight, who is your data really for? Is TSA a security agency or becoming part of a border enforcement network? Because if airport screening systems can be repurposed like this, what's next? This isn't about politics. It's about how systems evolve and how quickly the purpose of data can change. So, next time you fly, ask yourself, who already knows I'm coming? Comment below. Let me know what you think of this whole TSA ICE situation. this whole TSA ice coordination. And like always, I'm Brian, your flying mentor, and I'll catch you on the next one.
